body_txt: What's a fast way to clue the audience into a character's Super-Intelligence , either inborn or suddenly-gained? Why, give them a swollen (and often hairless) cranium, apparently housing a grossly enlarged brain. The head may or may not pulsate, sometimes more like a heart than a brain, and the rest of the body be comparatively weak and underdeveloped. Often related to Evolutionary Levels or dubious experiments done For Science! May involve the development of Psychic Powers if it's big enough . This trope has a few different "tiers" or variants. At the most basic form, a character will simply have a visibly large head or cranium. In more involved examples, the head may be fissured like a human brain, or the enlarged brain may become entirely exposed . In more extreme examples, the rest of the body may become withered and atrophied as the brain becomes bigger and more active, until at the furthest extreme of psychic and intellectual, a character or creature may become nothing but a giant engorged brain, sometimes retaining hands or eyes, and sometimes developing Psychic Powers so strong that these aren't even needed anymore. This is also sometimes used as a generic "alien trait" and not a specific indicator of intelligence. The Greys are a prominent example. While this may seem like Truth in Television , since animals with larger brains do tend to have a higher intellect than those with smaller cerebra, it turns out that intelligence is much more complicated than that (otherwise blue whales, with brains the size of fully-grown adult humans, would be the most intelligent species on the planet while African grey parrots, with brains the size of a walnut, wouldn't be anywhere near as intelligent as dolphins or chimpanzees). Turns out that intelligence is a function not so much of how large an organism's brain is, but how densely interconnected it is. The denser the brain tissue and the more neural pathways within an organism's brain, the higher its intellect. Additionally, not all parts of the brain scale in size together, and the parts of the cortex dedicated to conscious thinking or memory aren't necessarily going to increase in size with the rest of it. Among animals, for instance, birds tend to have more complexly "networked" brains than mammals do, while a lot of a whale's person-sized brain is taken up by the parts needed to run its even bigger body. Obviously a larger brain does have more room for connections and "intellectual" parts, but it's not as straightforward as this trope would have you believe. Fun fact: according to their autopsies, Michael Jackson, Whitney Houston, and Billy Mays all had bigger brains than Einstein! See also Forehead of Doom , which may be used to show this in a more realistic light, and Nurarihyon , a Yōkai species with a disproportionately large head who is often considered the supreme commander of yōkai. May sometimes overlap with Brain Monster if played for horror. No relation to Cranial Eruption . Contrast with Hollow-Sounding Head and Your Brain Won't Be Much of a Meal , both of which use a small brain as shorthand for stupidity.
